text: Description: Wget is a free, non-interactive utility for retrieving files using HTTP, HTTPS, and FTP protocols. It's designed for robustness over slow or unstable network connections and supports recursive downloads, link conversion, and file mirroring capabilities.
Category: Reconnaissance, Data Retrieval, Web Scraping
License: GNU General Public License v3.0 (GPL-3.0)
Operating Systems: Linux, macOS, Windows, BSD, and most UNIX-like systems
Minimal dependencies, runs on most systems with basic libraries
Or via Chocolatey: choco install wget
Or via Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)
Initial Setup: No specific initial setup required after installation.
Sample configuration entries: # Add a default header
no_proxy - Comma-separated list of domains to exclude from proxy
Default Credentials: Not applicable (credentials are supplied in command line or config file)
Non-interactive download of files from the Web
Support for HTTP, HTTPS, and FTP protocols
[URL] is the target URL to retrieve
--spider - Don't download, just check if file exists
Command Analysis: Reddit Server Response Headers
The command reveals Reddit's server information through HTTP response headers:
wget: A utility for retrieving content from web servers
--server-response: Displays the HTTP headers returned by the server
--spider: Makes wget behave like a web spider, not downloading content
grep -i: Filters output for specific case-insensitive patterns:
Description: Custom server name used by Reddit
Details: "snooserv" is a customized server name (named after Reddit's mascot "Snoo")
Security implications: The server doesn't disclose specific software or versions, which is a good security practice that prevents attackers from targeting known vulnerabilities
No "X-Runtime" header (would reveal application runtime information)
Limited Information Disclosure: Reddit follows security best practices by:
Using a custom server name that doesn't reveal implementation details
Not exposing backend technologies through common headers
Limited attack surface due to minimal information disclosure
Forces attackers to rely on more active probing techniques
No immediate version-specific vulnerabilities can be identified
Low: With only "snooserv" as information, an attacker cannot determine specific server software or versions
Next steps: Additional enumeration would be required:
Testing for common vulnerabilities regardless of platform
Related Techniques for Red Team Documentation
Try curl -I https://www.reddit.com for more complete header analysis
Use tools like whatweb for broader technology fingerprinting
Implement wafw00f to detect WAF (Web Application Firewall) presence
Custom server headers (as Reddit does) help reduce fingerprinting
Remove unnecessary headers that reveal technology stack
Configure web servers to minimize information disclosure
SSL/TLS analysis using sslscan or testssl.sh
DNS enumeration with dnsrecon or dnsenum
Port scanning with nmap using service detection
This output demonstrates proper security hardening of Reddit's servers by limiting information disclosure through HTTP headers.
The command reveals HTTP response headers from Cloudflare's website that indicate the presence of Cloudflare's security infrastructure.
Description: Explicitly identifies Cloudflare as the serving platform
Details: Indicates the site is behind Cloudflare's infrastructure
Security implications: Confirms the site leverages Cloudflare's CDN and security services
Purpose: Used to distinguish between humans and bots
Security role: Part of Cloudflare's bot detection and mitigation system
HttpOnly flag: Enhances security by preventing JavaScript access to cookies
Secure flag: Ensures cookies are only sent over HTTPS connections
SameSite=None: Allows cookies to be sent in cross-site requests (with Secure flag)
Description: Part of the Reporting API framework
Details: Contains endpoints for reporting specific issues to Cloudflare
Security implications: Allows browsers to send reports about potential security issues, errors, or performance problems back to Cloudflare
Multiple Cloudflare-specific headers indicate the site uses Cloudflare's security stack
The presence of bot management cookies suggests active traffic filtering
Connection details show traffic routing through Cloudflare IPs (104.16.123.96, 104.16.124.96)
While not explicitly labeled as "WAF" in headers, Cloudflare's presence implies their WAF is active
Bot management system indicated by cf_bm cookie is part of Cloudflare's layered security approach
Bypassing Cloudflare protection requires specialized techniques
Standard vulnerability scanning may trigger rate limiting or blocking
Direct IP attacks would be mitigated by Cloudflare's proxy
High: Cloudflare presents several challenges for attackers:
Origin server discovery may be necessary (finding the actual server behind Cloudflare)
Testing will likely require approaches that don't trigger Cloudflare's security mechanisms
Tools like Burp Suite may need special configurations to work effectively
Related Techniques for Red Team Documentation
Origin server identification (using historical DNS data, SSL certificate information)
Subdomain enumeration to find resources not behind Cloudflare
Using residential proxies to avoid IP reputation blocks
Gradual, low-volume scanning to avoid rate limiting
Testing for specific WAF bypass techniques
Using tools like wafw00f for WAF fingerprinting
Configure proper Page Rules for sensitive areas
Consider using Cloudflare Workers for custom security logic
This output demonstrates that the target site implements Cloudflare's security stack, which provides multiple layers of protection against common web attacks and unauthorized access attempts.
The command reveals that Wikipedia's web infrastructure uses NGINX version 1.22.1 as its web server.
Description: Explicitly identifies NGINX as the web server software with version 1.22.1
Details: NGINX is a popular high-performance web server and reverse proxy
Security implications: Exposes specific version information that could potentially be targeted for version-specific vulnerabilities
The header exposes the exact NGINX version (1.22.1)
This provides attackers with specific targeting information
Best practice would be to hide version information
Wikipedia uses NGINX, likely as a reverse proxy or load balancer
No explicit mention of other load balancers like HAProxy
The version (1.22.1) was released on August 2022, indicating regular but not cutting-edge updates
Version information allows for targeted vulnerability research
Attacker could look for CVEs specific to NGINX 1.22.1 or lower
Wikipedia's large-scale infrastructure likely includes additional layers not visible in headers
Medium: While version information is disclosed, NGINX 1.22.1 is relatively recent and major vulnerabilities would likely be patched
Attackers could research CVEs specific to this NGINX version
Configuration vulnerabilities are more likely than software vulnerabilities
Large organizations like Wikipedia typically have multiple security layers
Related Techniques for Red Team Documentation
Use tools like Wappalyzer or Nikto for broader technology stack identification
Attempt path traversal or unauthorized directory access
Test for SSI (Server Side Includes) injection if enabled
Look for custom error pages that might leak information
Remove or obscure server version information
Ensure regular patching of web server software
Use WAF rules to protect against common NGINX attack vectors
TLS/SSL configuration testing with tools like testssl.sh
HTTP method enumeration to check for non-standard methods
Testing for proxy configurations that might leak internal information

This output demonstrates that Wikipedia runs on NGINX 1.22.1, providing attackers with specific version information that could be used in targeted attacks, though as a major organization, Wikipedia likely has multiple security layers in place.
The command retrieves and analyzes the source code of MySQL.com, revealing relationships between MySQL and Oracle, as well as various web technologies used on the site.
Multiple references to Oracle in the code confirm that MySQL is owned by Oracle Corporation
Several Oracle legal links including privacy policy, terms of use, and trademark policy
Oracle JavaScript APIs used for consent management: oracle.truste.api.getGdprConsentDecision()
Main CSS files loaded from labs.mysql.com domain
Theme references suggest a design system called "sakila"
Chat functionality: oracle-chat.js with MySQL-specific configuration
Banner images specific to MySQL Summit 2025
Favicon and other brand assets hosted on labs.mysql.com
Clear integration between Oracle and MySQL platforms through:
Source code reveals underlying technologies and relationships
Multiple domains in use (www.mysql.com, labs.mysql.com, oracle.com)
Third-party services like consent.truste.com for GDPR compliance
Multiple domains provide expanded attack surface
JavaScript dependencies could be potential attack vectors
Cross-domain resource loading might have CORS or CSP implications
No database error messages found (good security practice)
No direct references to database technologies beyond MySQL
Oracle's ownership is prominently displayed throughout the codebase
Related Techniques for Red Team Documentation
Full JavaScript analysis for potential client-side vulnerabilities
Review of CSS for information leakage in selectors or comments
Implement subresource integrity checks for cross-domain resources
Review necessity of exposed organizational relationships in source code
Consider consolidating resources to reduce cross-domain dependencies

This analysis reveals that MySQL.com is owned by Oracle and uses various resources from both MySQL and Oracle domains. The site appears well-maintained with modern web technologies and strong integration between the MySQL brand and Oracle's infrastructure.
The command successfully downloaded Google's favicon.ico file, as confirmed by the subsequent ls command showing the file in the current directory.
wget: Utility for non-interactive downloading of files
-q (quiet): Suppresses wget's output, leaving no visual confirmation of success or failure
Target: Google's favicon (site icon) located at https://www.google.com/favicon.ico
The second command (ls) confirms successful download by showing "favicon.ico" file in the current directory.
Favicons are small icon files (typically 16×16 or 32×32 pixels) associated with websites
They appear in browser tabs, bookmarks, and history entries
Standard location is /favicon.ico in the website's root directory
Confirms the target website is operational
Validates that standard web paths are accessible
Can be used as a non-intrusive way to test connectivity
Provides insight into brand identity and site ownership
Low-noise reconnaissance technique (less likely to trigger alerts than aggressive scanning)
Can be used to verify target is operational without sending excessive requests
Demonstrates successful connection to target server
Confirms standard web paths are available
Shows server responds to direct file requests
Using -q reduces detection likelihood by minimizing network traffic
Simple file retrieval unlikely to trigger security alerts
Good preliminary step before more intrusive scanning
Some web servers may log direct requests to favicon.ico
Custom logging configurations might still detect this activity
Favicon fingerprinting can sometimes identify web server or CMS types
Related Techniques for Red Team Documentation
Check security.txt for security contact information
Review humans.txt for technology stack details
Extract metadata from favicon using ExifTool
Compare favicon hash against known CMS/framework fingerprints
Use tools like Shodan's favicon hash search for similar deployments
Bundle multiple file acquisitions in single sessions to reduce footprint
Use proxies or Tor for sensitive reconnaissance
Consider user-agent rotation to appear more like regular browser traffic
This simple favicon retrieval demonstrates basic, low-noise reconnaissance against Google's web infrastructure, confirming server accessibility and standard file path conventions.
The command retrieves HTTP response headers from Wikipedia.com, specifically examining and saving the cookies set by the server.
--save-cookies cookies.txt: Save cookies to specified file
--keep-session-cookies: Retain session cookies that would typically be discarded
grep -i "Set-Cookie": Filter results to only show cookie-setting headers
Purpose: Tracks user's last visit to Wikipedia
Security implications: Used for analytics and user experience, minimal security risk
Purpose: Tracks user's last visit across all Wikimedia Foundation sites
Security implications: Cross-domain tracking, broader scope than the first cookie
Security implications: Stores user's approximate location, potential privacy concern
Purpose: Likely related to rate limiting or network capability testing
Security implications: Configuration for anti-abuse mechanisms or performance optimization
All cookies use the Secure flag (HTTPS only)
Reasonable expiration periods (32 days for tracking, 1 hour for network probing)
GeoIP cookie reveals approximate user location
No sensitive identifiers or authentication tokens visible in these cookies
Cookie analysis reveals Wikimedia's user tracking mechanisms
Network security countermeasures partially revealed through configuration values
Related Techniques for Red Team Documentation
Test for insecure cookie handling (e.g., missing flags)
Attempt session fixation by manipulating cookies
Check for cross-site scripting vulnerabilities in cookie processing
Test impact of manipulating GeoIP cookie values
Determine if geo-restricted content can be accessed by cookie modification
Investigate relationship between actual IP and cookie-stored location
NetworkProbeLimit suggests anti-DoS or rate-limiting mechanisms
Cookie structure reveals aspects of backend architecture
Consider implementing SameSite=Strict for non-cross-site cookies
Review necessity of storing precise geolocation in cookies
Evaluate risks of exposing network security parameters

This analysis reveals that Wikipedia implements reasonably secure cookie practices with appropriate security flags, while providing insight into their tracking, geolocation, and network security mechanisms.
wget -q $(wget -q https://jquery.com -O - | grep -o "<script src=\".*\.js\"" | cut -d'"' -f2) -O - | grep -i "api\|endpoint\|config\|server\|database"
The command chain retrieves jQuery's JavaScript source code and searches for specific terms related to APIs, endpoints, configurations, server connections, and database references.
Retrieves the jQuery homepage HTML (wget -q https://jquery.com -O -)
Extracts JavaScript file URLs (grep -o "<script src=\".*\.js\"" | cut -d'"' -f2)
Searches the JavaScript for specified keywords
The output shows a snippet of jQuery's minified source code containing:

!function(e,t){"use strict";"object"==typeof module&&"object"==typeof module.exports?module.exports=e.document?(t(e,!0):function(e){if(!e.document)throw new Error("jQuery requires a window with a document");return t(e)}):t(e)}("undefined"!=typeof window?window:this,function(e,t){"use strict";var oe=[],r=Object.getPrototypeOf,ae=oe.slice,g=oe.flat?function(e){return oe.flat.call(e)}:function(e){return oe.concat.apply([],e)},s=oe.push,i=oe.indexOf,n={},o=n.toString,y=n.hasOwnProperty,a=y.toString,u=a.call(Object),v={},m=function(e){return"function"==typeof e&&"number"!=typeof e.nodeType&&"function"!=typeof e.item},x=function(e){return null!=e&&e===e.window},E=e.document,c={type:!0,src:!0,nonce:!0,noModule:!0},function m(e,t,n){var r,i,o=(n=n||E).createElement("script");if(o.text=e,t)for(r in c)(i=t[r])&&(o.setAttribute(r,i));n.head.appendChild(o).parentNode.removeChild(o)}function x(e){return null==e?e+"":"object"==typeof e||"function"==typeof e?n[i.call(e)]||"object":typeof e}var t="3.7.1",i=/HTML$/i,ce=function(e,t){return new ce.fn.init(e,t)};function ae(){...
The grep search found "api" within various JavaScript functions and method names
No explicit API endpoint URLs or configuration settings appear in this snippet
The term appears as part of function names and internal method references
Implementation uses an IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) pattern
Contains module loading logic for different environments (browser vs. Node.js)
Error handling for cases where a DOM document isn't available
Script handling includes security attributes like "nonce" (for CSP)
The code handles "noModule" attribute (modern browser feature detection)
Script creation functionality could be relevant for XSS vulnerability research
No configuration objects with sensitive data
No API keys or authentication tokens
jQuery is a client-side library with no direct server connections
No evidence of backend communication in the examined code
Primarily focused on DOM manipulation and JavaScript utilities
jQuery version information could be used to check for known vulnerabilities
Script injection capabilities might be leveraged if website implementation is flawed
No direct security vulnerabilities in the library code itself from this analysis
Related Techniques for Red Team Documentation
Check for outdated jQuery versions with known vulnerabilities
Identify jQuery plugins that may have security issues
Analyze how the website uses jQuery (especially dynamic content creation)
Examine how the application uses jQuery for AJAX requests
Look for custom jQuery extensions or plugins
Analyze event handlers for potential security issues
Keep jQuery updated to latest version
Use Subresource Integrity (SRI) for script loading
Validate all user input before processing with jQuery methods

This analysis shows that jQuery's core code does not contain hardcoded API endpoints, server configurations, or database connections, which aligns with its purpose as a client-side JavaScript library.
Solution: Use --no-check-certificate (security risk!) or update your CA certificates
Issue: Connection timeouts on slow connections
Solution: Increase timeout with --timeout=SECONDS or use --dns-timeout, --connect-timeout, and --read-timeout
Issue: No write permission for output file
Solution: Check directory permissions or use -P to specify a different output directory
Solution: Use -c to resume downloads or --tries=NUMBER to increase retry attempts
Use -d or --debug for verbose debugging output
Use -v for verbose (less than debug) output
Default User-Agent string identifies as Wget
Recursive downloads can be noisy and leave obvious patterns in logs
HTTP Basic Auth credentials in URLs can be logged on server side
Requests for non-linked resources in recursive mode
Set robots=off in wgetrc to avoid fetching robots.txt
Download with different filename: wget -O output.zip https://example.com/file.zip
Download recursively with depth: wget -r -l 2 https://example.com/
Curl - Command line tool for transferring data with URLs
HTTrack - Website copier and offline browser
wget examples on Unix & Linux Stack Exchange
